metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the '2-minute' time-based success criterion with an in-session-verifiable proxy, such as 'the GitHub issue template has ≤5 required fields and zero free-text-only prompts requiring domain knowledge'.
findings.finding: The success criterion 'An outsider can… report a bug on GitHub in under 2 minutes' sets a time-to-complete target that is not evaluable in-session via repo artifacts or a deterministic command — it requires actual user-testing with human subjects to validate.
findings.location: Success Criteria — bullet 1: 'An outsider can: report a bug on GitHub in under 2 minutes'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'The GitHub bug-report issue template requires no Gerrit or project-internal knowledge to complete: ≤5 fields, all with placeholder text, no field requiring knowledge of internal systems.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the 'Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' pre-flight experiment to a prerequisite that must complete before the shepherded-patch child story is opened, or qualify the 'authorship preserved' success criterion with 'if Forge Author ACL permits' to prevent the story from being written against an unverified capability.
findings.finding: The plan lists 'Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' as a pre-flight experiment (AC bullet 2) — explicitly acknowledging that the Forge Author capability is unverified — while simultaneously asserting in the Success Criteria that an outsider can 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback with authorship preserved)'. Authorship preservation via Gerrit's 'Forge Author' label is a non-default, administrator-granted ACL permission; whether it is enabled or can be safely granted to the bot/shepherd account is explicitly listed as unresolved.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 ('shepherded fallback with authorship preserved') and Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 ('Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' pre-flight experiment)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sequencing note: 'Forge Author ACL experiment must complete before the shepherded-patch story (child N) is claimed; if the ACL cannot be granted safely, the fallback degrades to maintainer-rewritten patch with Co-authored-by trailer instead of preserved Gerrit author.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Treat the Gerrit Forge Author ACL pre-flight experiment as a go/no-go gate for the 'authorship preserved' success criterion: verify that `forgeAuthor` can be granted in the rebar project.config on Gerrit 3.14 (probe via `ssh -p 29418 … gerrit ls-projects -p rebar --show-acls` or REST), and add a contingency path (e.g. 'Co-Authored-By trailer' fallback) if the ACL is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criteria unconditionally promise 'authorship preserved' for the shepherded-patch fallback, but the underlying Gerrit mechanism — the `forgeAuthor` ACL permission — is only listed as a pre-flight experiment (#2) with no go/no-go gate. The rebar project.config has no `forgeAuthor` grant today, and whether this permission can be added on the self-hosted Gerrit 3.14 instance has not been verified against the running server.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback with authorship preserved)'; Acceptance Criteria #2 — 'Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' pre-flight experiment

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe the pre-flight-experiment AC as an outcome-based criterion: e.g., 'each experiment's result is reflected in the corresponding child story's implementation decision (a repo artifact or documented policy), not only in a ticket comment'.
findings.finding: AC line 2 is effort-framed ('executed and outcomes recorded') rather than outcome-framed. 'Experiments executed' describes work performed, not a durable state change in the repo or system. The criterion is satisfied even if all four experiments reveal the assumed design is invalid and no design decision is updated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 2: 'The four pre-flight experiments… executed and outcomes recorded as ticket comments'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign one child (or add a completion step to child 1eb1 as the CONTRIBUTING.md restructure owner) to perform and record a final consistency audit of CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, your-first-change.md, review-policy.md, GOVERNANCE.md, and MAINTAINERS.md after all siblings have landed.
findings.finding: No child owns a final integration consistency audit of CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, and all new docs as required by parent AC4 ('No new artifact contradicts the Gerrit-primary workflow — CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, and the new docs stay mutually consistent'). Children 1eb1, 3b51, ddcd, and 1689 each independently add or restructure sections of CONTRIBUTING.md, but none is assigned responsibility for verifying the assembled result is mutually consistent.
findings.location: child 1eb1-ab18-0e97-43c8; child 3b51-5bff-2d41-44c8; child ddcd-fc9e-a81f-4cbb; child 1689-6a5c-7896-4455
findings.suggested_fix: Add an acceptance criterion to child 1eb1 (as CONTRIBUTING.md restructure owner): 'After all sibling stories that touch CONTRIBUTING.md (3b51, ddcd) and CLAUDE.md (ddcd) have landed, a final pass confirms no mutual contradiction exists across CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, docs/your-first-change.md, and docs/review-policy.md; result recorded on the parent ticket.'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the DCO mechanism for the shepherded-patch case: state whether the workflow requires both the original author's and the maintainer's Signed-off-by trailers, who adds each, and how the Gerrit gate is configured to check for the correct set.
findings.finding: The DCO enforcement mechanism for the shepherded-patch path has a hidden interaction gap: Gerrit server-side DCO (receive.requireSignedOffBy = true) requires a Signed-off-by trailer from the original author, but in the shepherded model a maintainer submits on behalf of an external contributor. The plan does not specify whether the shepherded-patch workflow ensures both the original author's SOB and the maintainer's SOB are present, who is responsible for adding them, or whether any gate checks for their presence. OpenStack's DCO model (the named peer convention) requires both trailers on co-authored changes; the plan adopts the peer convention's conclusion without specifying whether the mechanism handles the two-SOB case.
findings.location: Context section — 'enforce DCO at the Gerrit server (OpenStack's 2025 CLA→DCO move)'; Success Criteria — 'Governance, review policy, DCO, and maintainer info are published … grounded in a named peer convention'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate docs/review-policy.md (child 1689) as the single authoritative source for the vote-semantics taxonomy; update child 1eb1's acceptance criteria to require that your-first-change.md summarizes and links to review-policy.md rather than independently restating the full table.
findings.finding: Child 1eb1 (your-first-change.md) and child 1689 (review-policy.md) both document the vote-semantics taxonomy (BLOCK—finding vs BLOCK—coverage-gap vs Verified −1) in separate documents with no deduplication protocol. Divergence in wording across the two documents will give contributors inconsistent information about the same gate.
findings.suggested_fix: In child 1eb1's your-first-change.md, replace the inline vote-semantics descriptions with a 2-sentence summary and a 'See docs/review-policy.md for the full vote-semantics table' pointer. Add this as an AC to child 1eb1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of the docs/release-notes.md cross-link to child ed57 (move it from OUT to IN scope), or explicitly assign it to another child.
findings.finding: Child ed57 states that `docs/release-notes.md` should receive a cross-link only ('cross-link only' per scope), but no child in the roster owns adding that cross-link to docs/release-notes.md. This is an owned-by-none gap for a named deliverable.
findings.location: child ed57-3657-6d0a-4cca
findings.suggested_fix: Move 'add a cross-link from docs/release-notes.md to CHANGELOG.md (one sentence: see CHANGELOG.md for the user-facing version history)' into child ed57's scope IN list and add it as an acceptance criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note (or a thin precursor task) that the four pre-flight experiments must be completed and their outcomes recorded before the dependent children (PR posture, DCO, review policy) begin implementation, to prevent mid-flight redesign.
findings.finding: The four pre-flight experiments (has_pull_requests UX, Gerrit Forge Author ACL, PR-event dependency audit, rebar-ticket trailer policy) are listed as parent ACs but no sequencing gate separates them from the 8 child tickets; children nonsecular-arthritic-sidewinder (PR posture), sepia-cardiac-hawk (DCO), and wrathful-preregal-blobfish (review policy) depend on experiment outcomes, so parallel execution risks rework if an experiment invalidates a child's design assumption.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — pre-flight experiments bullet (#2); children nonsecular-arthritic-sidewinder, sepia-cardiac-hawk, wrathful-preregal-blobfish
findings.suggested_fix: In the parent ticket description or AC #2, add: 'Pre-flight experiments MUST be marked complete (outcomes recorded as ticket comments) before starting nonsecular-arthritic-sidewinder, sepia-cardiac-hawk, and wrathful-preregal-blobfish.' Optionally promote the experiments to a sequenced precursor child ticket that gates the remaining seven.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verifiable citation (URL or dated source) for each of the two peer-convergence claims: the Wikimedia GitLab-exit reversal and the JGit→GerritHub migration, so downstream governance docs can cite them accurately.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Wikimedia reversed its GitLab exit in 2024 over the review model' and 'JGit migrated TO GerritHub rather than accept PRs' as peer-convergence evidence for the Gerrit-primary design, but neither claim has a citation or source document anywhere in the repo. If either is inaccurate, the foundational peer-convergence argument for the Gerrit wall is weakened.
findings.location: Context section — 'Peer convergence establishes the design: KEEP Gerrit (Wikimedia reversed its GitLab exit in 2024 over the review model; JGit migrated TO GerritHub rather than accept PRs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Sources' subsection to the Context (matching the pattern in docs/oss-comparison-and-remediation.md) with direct links, e.g. the Wikimedia Phabricator task or mailing-list thread, and the JGit project's hosting announcement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the 'OpenStack 2025 CLA→DCO move' claim (date and event) and replace with a correct, cited reference — or substitute a different, verifiable 2025-era peer that adopted DCO (e.g. a specific CNCF project if one applies).
findings.finding: The plan names 'OpenStack's 2025 CLA→DCO move' as the grounding peer convention for enforcing DCO at the Gerrit server, but OpenStack's CLA→DCO transition is historically associated with 2017-2018 (OpenStack Foundation governance change), not 2025. A 'search_files' for 'OpenStack 2025 CLA DCO' returns zero matches in the repo. If the date is wrong, the cited peer is not evidence of 2025-era convergence.
findings.location: Context section — 'enforce DCO at the Gerrit server (OpenStack's 2025 CLA→DCO move)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that git-cliff can generate CHANGELOG output compatible with rebar's existing commit-message format (rebar-ticket trailers, not Conventional Commits) and document the required cliff.toml template configuration, or name an alternative tool if git-cliff is incompatible.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criteria state that 'Release artifacts … match 2026 Python-tool convergence (git-cliff generate-then-curate …)' but git-cliff is not configured in the repo (no cliff.toml, no .cliff.toml, no git-cliff reference in pyproject.toml or any workflow), and the plan does not resolve whether git-cliff can produce CHANGELOG output compatible with the existing docs/release-notes.md format and the release.yml auto-generated notes workflow. This is an unverified capability assertion for a tool that requires non-trivial configuration.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Release artifacts (CHANGELOG, dependabot, README front door, 1.0 declaration mechanics) match 2026 Python-tool convergence (git-cliff generate-then-curate; github-actions-only dependabot; classifier bump; no tag-signing ceremony)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a git-cliff integration spec to the CHANGELOG child story: name the cliff.toml commit-parser config, the release.yml step (or standalone script) that invokes it, and verify the `git cliff generate` / `git cliff --tag` subcommand surface against the installed binary before implementing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'git-cliff generate-then-curate' as the CHANGELOG tool meeting '2026 Python-tool convergence,' but git-cliff has zero prior use in this codebase — no cliff.toml, no workflow step, no dev-dependency, no release-runbook mention. The specific CLI surface (subcommands, config schema, integration with the existing release.yml tag-push flow) is completely unverified.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Release artifacts … match 2026 Python-tool convergence (git-cliff generate-then-curate …)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CHANGELOG child story, add a concrete integration note: install git-cliff as a dev dependency (`pip install git-cliff` or via cargo/brew), author a `cliff.toml` with the project's conventional-commit prefix map, and add a release-runbook step (`git cliff --tag vX.Y.Z -o docs/CHANGELOG.md`) verified against `git cliff --help` output. Wire into release.yml as a pre-tag step or document it as a manual curator step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Establish a merge order for CONTRIBUTING.md edits: child 1eb1 (restructure) lands LAST among the four children that edit it, after children 3b51 and ddcd have merged their sections; or designate child 1eb1 to incorporate the other children's sections directly.
findings.finding: Four children (1eb1, 3b51, ddcd, 1689) all write to CONTRIBUTING.md concurrently with no declared merge order, conflict-resolution protocol, or sequencing constraint. This is an implicit shared-state conflict that can cause one child's edits to silently overwrite or displace another's.
findings.location: child 1eb1-ab18-0e97-43c8; child 3b51-5bff-2d41-44c8; child ddcd-fc9e-a81f-4cbb; child 1689-6a5c-7896-4455
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ordering note to child 1eb1: 'CONTRIBUTING.md restructure change must be submitted to Gerrit after children 3b51 (shepherding section) and ddcd (DCO section) have merged, and must include both sections in their correct positions within the restructured layout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit ordering constraints: children 1689 and 3b51 must not merge deliverables that link to docs/your-first-change.md until child 1eb1 has merged that file.
findings.finding: Children 1689 and 3b51 both create links to `docs/your-first-change.md` (owned by child 1eb1) without declaring a dependency on child 1eb1 completing first. If either child closes before child 1eb1 creates the file, the links are dangling.
findings.suggested_fix: In children 1689 and 3b51, add a dependency note: 'Blocked on child 1eb1 (your-first-change.md) merging first.' Alternatively, the lockdown bot comment can initially link to CONTRIBUTING.md and be updated after child 1eb1 merges.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Surface child ddcd's migration-order constraint as a cross-child dependency: child 1eb1's CONTRIBUTING.md restructure must land BEFORE child ddcd's server-flag flip, and child 1eb1 must preserve (not overwrite) the DCO section added by child ddcd.
findings.finding: Child ddcd's strict internal migration order (docs land → agent tooling updated → server flag flipped) is invisible to sibling child 1eb1, which is simultaneously restructuring CONTRIBUTING.md (the primary docs artifact in ddcd's first phase). A concurrent CONTRIBUTING.md restructure from child 1eb1 could land between ddcd's doc phase and its server-flip phase, overwriting the DCO section and creating a window where the server is enforcing DCO but CONTRIBUTING.md no longer documents it.
findings.location: child ddcd-fc9e-a81f-4cbb; child 1eb1-ab18-0e97-43c8
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child ddcd's acceptance criteria: 'Confirmed that child 1eb1's CONTRIBUTING.md restructure has been reviewed to preserve the DCO section in its final form before the server flag is flipped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback path to child 3b51: if E3 finds a pull_request-event dependency in existing workflows, the child's scope expands to include the necessary workflow fix (or a new sub-task is created and linked before child 3b51 closes).
findings.finding: If pre-flight experiment E3 (audit of existing GitHub Actions for pull_request-event dependencies) finds a workflow that breaks due to the lockdown posture change, no child in the roster owns the fix. Child 3b51's scope explicitly covers the audit and recording the outcome, but fixing a discovered dependency is out of scope for all eight children.
findings.location: child 3b51-5bff-2d41-44c8
findings.suggested_fix: In child 3b51's acceptance criteria, add: 'If E3 finds a pull_request-event dependency in any existing workflow, the affected workflow is patched and the fix is merged before the lockdown workflow lands; outcome recorded on the ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of the four pre-flight experiments, state the expected outcome AND a fallback design decision if the experiment yields a negative result, so the executor never needs to stop for clarification.
findings.finding: The four 'pre-flight experiments' in AC line 2 are 'verify whether'/'check if' patterns that defer real design decisions to the executor. For example, if the 'fork-side UX with has_pull_requests=false' experiment fails, no fallback PR posture is specified; if the 'Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' reveals the ACL cannot be configured as assumed, no alternative DCO enforcement path is given. An executor reaching a negative result has no plan-specified path forward.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 2: 'The four pre-flight experiments… executed and outcomes recorded as ticket comments'
findings.suggested_fix: For each experiment, add an 'if yes → proceed with X; if no → fall back to Y' branch inline. E.g.: 'fork-side UX: if has_pull_requests=false suppresses fork UI, keep; if not, add a NOTICE banner to CONTRIBUTING.md directing users away from forking.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the authorship-preservation mechanism for shepherded patches (e.g., `git commit --author='Contributor <email>'` plus maintainer `Signed-off-by`, or a named alternative) so the executor does not need to choose.
findings.finding: The mechanism for 'authorship preserved' in the shepherded-patch fallback is unspecified. An executor must choose whether to use `git commit --author`, a `Co-authored-by` trailer, a `Patch-by` Gerrit custom field, or some other approach — this is a real design decision, not an obvious convention.
findings.location: Context: 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback with authorship preserved)'; AC bullet 2: 'rebar-ticket trailer policy for shepherded patches'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the shepherded-patch description: 'Maintainer sets `--author` to the contributor's name/email from the GitHub PR, adds their own `Signed-off-by` for DCO, and includes a `Co-authored-by` trailer. This satisfies Gerrit's Forge Author ACL and the DCO server hook.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'staged' with a specific observable artifact: e.g., 'a draft PR exists that includes both the `docs/api-stability.md` caveat removal and the `Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable` classifier, gated by a `do-not-merge/wait-for-1.0` label'.
findings.finding: AC line 3 uses 'staged' as the observable artifact state, which is ambiguous — it could mean a branch exists, a draft PR exists, a commit is cherry-picked, or changes are in a checklist for a future PR. An executor cannot deterministically verify 'staged' via a repo command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 3: 'The `docs/api-stability.md` pre-1.0 caveat flip and the Development Status classifier bump are staged for the v1.0.0 release change'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'A draft PR (labeled `release/1.0`) exists containing: (a) removal of the pre-1.0 caveat block in `docs/api-stability.md` and (b) `Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable` in `pyproject.toml` classifiers. The PR CI passes but is not merged until the v1.0.0 tag is cut.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description of CLAUDE.md (what it is, what it currently says about workflow) in the Context or a child story, and ensure AC[4]'s consistency check is grounded in described work for that file.
findings.finding: CLAUDE.md is referenced in AC[4] as a consistency target ('CONTRIBUTING.md, CLAUDE.md, and the new docs stay mutually consistent') but is never mentioned or described anywhere else in the plan — neither in Context, Success Criteria, nor as a deliverable. There is no described work covering what CLAUDE.md is, what it currently contains, or what (if any) changes are required to it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC[4]
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context section, add one sentence identifying CLAUDE.md (e.g., 'CLAUDE.md is the AI-assistant instructions file; it currently references Gerrit onboarding commands that must be updated to match the new public contribution path'). Optionally add an explicit sub-task under the onboarding docs child story to audit and update CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a top-level AC (or make explicit in AC[1]'s description) that GitHub Issues on navapbc/rebar are enabled and a bug-report template is present, directly grounding the '2-minute bug report' success criterion.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria states 'An outsider can: report a bug on GitHub in under 2 minutes' — implying GitHub Issues must be enabled — but no AC directly verifies that GitHub Issues (and/or Discussions) are enabled on the navapbc/rebar repository. The only potentially covering criterion is AC[1]'s 'GitHub intake child story closed,' but the plan does not spell out what the GitHub intake child story's own ACs include, leaving a gap in verifiability at this plan level.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) and Acceptance Criteria AC[1]
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC[1]: 'including GitHub Issues enabled with a bug-report template on navapbc/rebar' — or add a standalone AC: '[ ] GitHub Issues are enabled on navapbc/rebar and a filled bug-report template is present (verifiable via GitHub API).'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a conditional branch to the shepherded-patch success criterion: specify what the plan delivers if the Forge Author ACL experiment returns negative (e.g., commit-message attribution as a fallback), so the criterion is achievable regardless of the experiment outcome.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion guarantees 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback with authorship preserved)' as a delivered outcome, but authorship preservation via the Forge Author ACL is listed only as an experiment to be verified. If the Gerrit Forge Author ACL check (pre-flight experiment #2) returns a negative result — i.e., the ACL is unavailable or not grantable in the rebar project config — the mechanism that makes authorship preservation possible simply does not exist, yet the Success Criterion is written unconditionally. The plan provides no contingency path for the negative experiment outcome.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback with authorship preserved)'; Acceptance Criteria #2 — 'Gerrit Forge Author ACL check' pre-flight experiment
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the success criterion as: 'submit a small patch with zero Gerrit setup (shepherded fallback; authorship preserved via Forge Author ACL if available, else via `Co-authored-by`/`Original-Author:` trailer)' — and add a note that the DCO child story specifies which attribution mechanism applies based on the experiment result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Gerrit server-side DCO enforcement mechanism in the DCO child story (e.g. `uploadvalidator` plugin with `requireSignedOffBy=true`, a custom Prolog submit rule, or a `commit-msg` hook deployed via Gerrit's server-side hook mechanism), verify it is compatible with Gerrit 3.14, and add any required Dockerfile/config changes to the story's scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'enforce DCO at the Gerrit server' as a design choice (citing OpenStack's 2025 CLA→DCO move) but names no specific Gerrit plugin or submit-rule mechanism for DCO enforcement. Gerrit's core does not natively validate Signed-off-by trailers; a dedicated plugin (e.g. a custom submit rule, the `uploadvalidator` plugin, or a `commit-msg` hook enforcing DCO) is required. None is present in the committed Gerrit config or Dockerfile.
findings.location: Context — 'enforce DCO at the Gerrit server (OpenStack's 2025 CLA→DCO move)'; implied by the DCO child story in Acceptance Criteria #1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the new PR auto-answer bot workflow's GitHub Actions trigger event (`pull_request` preferred over `pull_request_target`) and its GITHUB_TOKEN permission scope (minimum required, e.g., `pull-requests: write` only), and extend the PR-event dependency audit pre-flight to cover this new workflow explicitly.
findings.finding: The new PR auto-answer bot (a GitHub Actions workflow triggered by public pull-request events on the GitHub mirror) has an undeclared access posture: the plan does not state which GitHub Actions trigger event it uses (`pull_request` vs `pull_request_target`), what GITHUB_TOKEN permission scope it holds, or whether it ever writes back to the repository context — all of which determine whether a malicious PR can escalate into a poisoned-pipeline or secret-exfiltration attack. The pre-flight PR-event audit explicitly covers *existing* workflows only, leaving the new bot uncovered.
findings.location: Context / Acceptance Criteria — 'bot-answered PRs' and 'PR-event dependency audit of existing GitHub Actions'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the `pull_request` trigger (not `pull_request_target`) for the bot workflow so it runs in the fork's restricted context with no access to base-branch secrets; if a comment/label write-back is needed, use a separate `workflow_run` step that fires after the untrusted code has already been evaluated in the sandboxed context. Pin all third-party actions to a full commit SHA. Set `permissions: pull-requests: write` (and nothing else) at the job level and declare this in the child story for the PR posture epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either create docs/plan-review-gate.md as a deliverable within child 1689's scope, or replace the link with a prose note ('the plan-review gate is documented separately for internal contributors') that does not reference a non-existent file.
findings.finding: Child 1689 references `docs/plan-review-gate.md` in a clarifying sentence ('link to docs/plan-review-gate.md'), but no child in the complete sibling roster creates or owns this file. This is an owned-by-none residual reference that will produce a broken link in the published review-policy.md.
findings.suggested_fix: In child 1689, update the What section: replace 'link to docs/plan-review-gate.md' with 'note that the plan-review gate is an internal workflow; no external link is needed in v1.' If docs/plan-review-gate.md is a desired artifact, add it to child 1689's scope IN list and acceptance criteria.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
